items.reality: Only if every package (including transitive ones) is pinned to exact versions, and ideally with hashes; ranges still allow drift.
items.example: `requests>=2.31` may install 2.31.0 today and 2.32.4 next week. For *true* reproducibility, use `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (or a real lockfile) so every transitive pin is locked + checksummed.

items.myth: pip freeze is the right way to author requirements.
items.reality: freeze is a snapshot of all installed versions; it's better as a generated lock than a hand-maintained list of direct dependencies.
items.example: `pip freeze` of a small project: 50+ lines mixing your 3 direct deps with 47 transitives. Hand-edit that and intent is lost in noise. Curate `requirements.in`; compile to `requirements.txt`.

items.myth: pip resolves the best version set like a full solver.
items.reality: Modern pip has a real resolver, but each install resolves against the current index state; without pins, results can change over time.
items.example: `pip install fastapi pydantic` today resolves to a working pair; if pydantic ships v3 next month with API changes, the same command picks an incompatible combination. Pins make the resolved set permanent.
